questions.question: In the context of the story's opening, what does the phrase 'ticking off items in a long list' primarily suggest about Mr. Shamnath's character?
options: His professional background as an accountant or clerk.
His obsessive-compulsive nature regarding cleanliness.
His anxiety to perform a flawless display of social status.
His lack of trust in his wife's ability to manage the household.
questions.correct: His anxiety to perform a flawless display of social status.
questions.explanation: The list-making highlights his desperation to impress his superior and his view of the dinner as a performance of his professional and social standing.

questions.question: Why does Mr. Shamnath reject his wife's suggestion to send his mother to the neighbor's house for the night?
options: He wants his mother to meet the American boss to show off her traditional values.
He is concerned that it would facilitate a social connection he wishes to avoid.
He is genuinely concerned about his mother's comfort in an unfamiliar house.
He fears the neighbor will discover their lack of hospitality.
questions.correct: He is concerned that it would facilitate a social connection he wishes to avoid.
questions.explanation: He refers to the neighbor as a 'hag' and fears that if the mother stays there, the neighbor will start visiting their home again.

questions.question: The mother's refusal to eat when 'flesh is cooked' serves to highlight which literary contrast?
options: The lack of communication between the husband and the wife regarding the menu.
The conflict between poverty and the sudden abundance of food for guests.
The generational divide between traditional dietary laws and modern lifestyle.
The mother's passive-aggressive attempt to ruin the dinner party.
questions.correct: The generational divide between traditional dietary laws and modern lifestyle.
questions.explanation: Her adherence to religious/traditional vegetarianism contrasts with the son's modern, Westernized party involving meat and alcohol.

questions.question: What is the significance of the mother wearing a white kameez and salwar for the party?
options: It matches the décor that the boss's wife eventually admires.
It symbolizes her role as a widow and her son's attempt to 'standardize' her appearance.
It represents her joy and celebration for her son's impending promotion.
It was her most expensive outfit, reserved for special occasions.
questions.correct: It symbolizes her role as a widow and her son's attempt to 'standardize' her appearance.
questions.explanation: Shamnath chooses the outfit to make her look presentable and 'disciplined' according to his own meticulous, controlling standards.

questions.question: When Shamnath tells his mother not to 'make a song' about her sold jewellery, what does this reveal about his psychological state?
options: He is suffering from deep-seated guilt and reacts with defensive aggression.
He believes that financial repayment is the only valid form of gratitude.
He is trying to keep his mother focused on the immediate task of dressing up.
He is genuinely forgetful of the sacrifices made for his education.
questions.correct: He is suffering from deep-seated guilt and reacts with defensive aggression.
questions.explanation: His sharp retort to her sacrifice suggests he feels the weight of the debt and chooses to lash out rather than acknowledge the emotional cost.

questions.question: Which word best describes the tone of the boss's interaction with the mother in the verandah?
options: Condescending
Indifferent
Amiable
Patronizing
questions.correct: Amiable
questions.explanation: The boss saves the awkward situation by being friendly, shaking her hand, and expressing interest in village life.

questions.question: What is the irony regarding the 'phulkari' mentioned in the later half of the story?
options: The mother is actually unable to make a phulkari because she is from a city.
The phulkari is a symbol of mourning, yet the boss sees it as a celebratory item.
The boss values the craft while Shamnath views it merely as a tool for promotion.
Shamnath hates traditional crafts but has to pretend to love them.
questions.correct: The boss values the craft while Shamnath views it merely as a tool for promotion.
questions.explanation: The son, who wanted to hide his 'village' mother, finds that her 'village' skills are exactly what his boss admires most.

questions.question: The mother's desire to go to Hardwar is a recurring motif that represents:
options: Her desire to escape the physical labor of the household.
Her search for spiritual peace and a graceful exit from a world she no longer fits.
Her interest in seeing the world beyond her son's suburban home.
A tactic to make Shamnath feel guilty for his poor treatment of her.
questions.correct: Her search for spiritual peace and a graceful exit from a world she no longer fits.
questions.explanation: Hardwar is a holy city where many go for meditation in their final years, symbolizing her wish to detach from her son's materialistic life.

questions.question: What is the primary reason the mother eventually agrees to make the phulkari for the boss?
options: She hopes it will earn her the money she needs to travel to Hardwar.
Her self-sacrificing nature is triggered by the prospect of her son's promotion.
She is flattered by the boss's interest in her work.
She wants to prove to her daughter-in-law that she is still useful.
questions.correct: Her self-sacrificing nature is triggered by the prospect of her son's promotion.
questions.explanation: As soon as she hears that pleasing the boss might lead to a 'lift' or higher post for her son, she ignores her failing eyesight to help him.

questions.question: In the sentence 'Mr. Shamnath's dinner had reached the crescendo of success,' the word 'crescendo' implies:
options: The moment when the mother started her snoring.
The formal introduction of the main course.
A noisy and chaotic ending to the party.
The point of highest intensity or peak of the evening.
questions.correct: The point of highest intensity or peak of the evening.
questions.explanation: The term suggests the party has built up successfully to a maximum level of enjoyment and approval from the guests.

questions.question: What does Shamnath's reaction to the mother's snoring ('seethed with anger') reveal about his priorities?
options: He is concerned about his mother's health and breathing difficulties.
He is frustrated because he told her specifically to stay awake.
He is worried that the noise will wake up the neighbors.
He prioritizes the aesthetic and social 'perfection' of his home over human empathy.
questions.correct: He prioritizes the aesthetic and social 'perfection' of his home over human empathy.
questions.explanation: His anger stems from her ruining the 'image' he tried to create for his boss, treating her like a defective piece of furniture.

questions.question: How does the boss's wife serve as a character foil to Shamnath's mother?
options: She is unfriendly and cold, making the mother appear more welcoming.
She is an American, while the mother represents the typical Indian middle class.
She represents the modernity and high social standing that Shamnath aspires to, contrasting with his mother's traditionalism.
She is more religious and traditional than the mother, showing Shamnath's poor judgment.
questions.correct: She represents the modernity and high social standing that Shamnath aspires to, contrasting with his mother's traditionalism.
questions.explanation: Her gown, pearls, perfume, and 'free' social manner contrast sharply with the mother's simple clothes, beads, and timid silence.

questions.question: Which detail suggests that Shamnath is a 'stickler for discipline' in a way that borders on domestic tyranny?
options: He has the final say on everything from curtain colors to which sari his wife should wear.
He locks the door from the outside to keep his mother in her room.
He makes a long list and smokes many cigarettes while working.
He insists on serving Indian dishes to the American boss.
questions.correct: He has the final say on everything from curtain colors to which sari his wife should wear.
questions.explanation: The text lists his control over every minute detail of the house and his wife's clothing as evidence of his meticulous and controlling nature.

questions.question: The 'pomegranate song' incident is crucial because it represents:
options: The transition from viewing the mother as an embarrassment to viewing her as a cultural commodity.
The mother's hidden talent as a professional singer.
The moment Shamnath realizes his mother is a burden on his career.
A failure in Shamnath's planning because she forgot the lyrics.
questions.correct: The transition from viewing the mother as an embarrassment to viewing her as a cultural commodity.
questions.explanation: The applause from the guests and the boss's delight change Shamnath's anger into joy as he sees his mother 'introduced a new note into the party.'

questions.question: What is the implied meaning of 'I wash my hands of this affair' as spoken by Shamnath's wife?
options: She is resigning from her duties as a housewife.
She is literally washing her hands because she was cleaning the bric-a-brac.
She is refusing to be blamed for any future conflict between Shamnath and his mother.
She is going to take a bath and get ready for the party.
questions.correct: She is refusing to be blamed for any future conflict between Shamnath and his mother.
questions.explanation: The phrase is used to signal that she will not participate in the decision-making or the potential 'bad name' resulting from it.

questions.question: The phrase 'reduced to ashes' in the mother's dialogue is an example of:
options: A literal curse she is placing on herself.
A hint that there will be a fire later in the story.
Her anger at the way her son is treating her.
Hyperbolic traditional expression of regret.
questions.correct: Hyperbolic traditional expression of regret.
questions.explanation: It is a dramatic, culturally specific way of saying she regrets speaking in a way that her son misunderstood as asking for money.

questions.question: In the context of the story, why is the phulkari being 'threadbare' significant?
options: It reflects the mother's own aging and the neglect of her traditional world.
It is the reason Shamnath decides not to show it to the boss.
It proves that the mother was a bad needlewoman.
It shows that the family was too poor to buy new things.
questions.correct: It reflects the mother's own aging and the neglect of her traditional world.
questions.explanation: The crumbling cloth symbolizes the fading of the old ways of life that Shamnath has ignored until they became useful for his promotion.

questions.question: What does the boss mean when he says he likes 'village folk'?
options: He is trying to make Shamnath feel ashamed of his urban lifestyle.
He wants to move his office to a rural area.
He thinks they are more efficient workers than city people.
He views them as an exotic cultural curiosity or source of authentic craft.
questions.correct: He views them as an exotic cultural curiosity or source of authentic craft.
questions.explanation: His interest is in the songs, dances, and 'things which the women make themselves,' treating the culture as a fascinating artifact.

questions.question: The mother's reaction of 'tears... like monsoon showers' after the guests leave indicates:
options: Her fear that she will be punished for her clumsy handshake.
Her immense joy at her son's success.
A release of long-suppressed emotional pain and the trauma of being treated as an obstacle.
Her regret at having sold her jewellery in the past.
questions.correct: A release of long-suppressed emotional pain and the trauma of being treated as an obstacle.
questions.explanation: The 'flood-gates of years of old pent-up feelings' implies she has been holding in the hurt of her son's dismissiveness for a long time.

questions.question: Shamnath's behavior of 'throwing up his hands in irritation' and 'seething with anger' suggests he views his mother primarily as:
options: A useful domestic helper for his wife.
A source of traditional wisdom for the guests.
A beloved elder who needs protection.
A logistical obstacle to his professional ambitions.
questions.correct: A logistical obstacle to his professional ambitions.
questions.explanation: Every interaction with her is focused on how she might 'spoil the whole thing' or 'offend the boss.'

questions.question: Bhisham Sahni's story is a critique of which social phenomenon?
options: The hypocrisy and heartlessness of the upwardly mobile middle class.
The lack of educational facilities for the rural poor.
The decline of the Indian textile industry.
The difficulties faced by Americans in understanding Indian culture.
questions.correct: The hypocrisy and heartlessness of the upwardly mobile middle class.
questions.explanation: The story exposes how Shamnath values his career more than his mother's dignity and only values her when she serves his interests.

questions.question: Why does Shamnath insist his mother should not wear her wooden sandals?
options: He thinks they are uncomfortable for her to stand in.
They don't match the new white kameez he chose.
They make too much noise on the verandah floors.
The boss's wife is wearing modern heels, and he wants her to match.
questions.correct: They make too much noise on the verandah floors.
questions.explanation: Wooden sandals ('kharaun') are noisy and associated with traditional/rural life, which Shamnath finds embarrassing for his 'modern' dinner.

questions.question: The boss's interest in the phulkari 'which the women make themselves' contrasts with Shamnath's mention of:
options: The medals Shamnath won in college.
The Western gowns worn by the female guests.
The 'bazaar things' that the boss specifically rejects.
The Indian dishes cooked for dinner.
questions.correct: The 'bazaar things' that the boss specifically rejects.
questions.explanation: The boss wants authentic home-made items, whereas Shamnath initially struggles to think beyond commercial products or basic dolls.

questions.question: In the final scene, when Shamnath hugs his mother and calls her 'good Ammi,' his affection is primarily driven by:
options: Relief and greed regarding his potential promotion.
Alcohol consumption during the party.
Guilt for having almost locked her in her room.
A sudden realization of how much he loves her.
questions.correct: Relief and greed regarding his potential promotion.
questions.explanation: He is happy because 'the Sahib was so pleased,' which he believes will lead to his career advancement.

questions.question: The mother's confusion during the handshake — placing her left hand in the Sahib's right — serves as a symbol for:
options: The incompatibility of her traditional, religious life with her son's Westernized world.
The boss's misunderstanding of Indian etiquette.
Her lack of coordination due to old age.
Her attempt to insult the boss for his foreign ways.
questions.correct: The incompatibility of her traditional, religious life with her son's Westernized world.
questions.explanation: She cannot shake with her right hand because it is occupied with her prayer beads, symbolizing how her spiritual life is literally 'in the way' of her son's social requirements.

questions.question: What is the 'rat race' Shamnath refers to in the final conversation?
options: A traditional Punjabi game he is explaining to his mother.
The rush to finish the phulkari before the boss returns.
The fierce and competitive struggle for professional promotion.
The literal presence of rodents in his mother's room.
questions.correct: The fierce and competitive struggle for professional promotion.
questions.explanation: It describes the cutthroat environment of his office where everyone is competing for the boss's favor.

questions.question: Whom does Mr. Shamnath address as the 'next-door hag'?
options: The mother
The neighbour
The boss's wife
questions.correct: The neighbour

questions.question: According to Mr. Shamnath, the guests won't start arriving before ____
options: Seven o'clock
Nine o'clock
Eight o'clock
questions.correct: Eight o'clock

questions.question: Who said the lines: 'But if she falls asleep and starts snoring! Then?'
options: Mr. Shamnath
Mrs. Shamnath
The neighbors
questions.correct: Mrs. Shamnath

questions.question: The mother was going to visit her brother, but Mrs. Shamnath interfered because she wanted to:
options: Keep up appearances before her relatives
Keep the mother at home for her own company
Keep up appearances in front of her friends
questions.correct: Keep up appearances in front of her friends

questions.question: The mother's room opened onto the ____
options: Garden
Verandah
Bathroom
questions.correct: Verandah

questions.question: Who among the following does not eat when flesh is cooked in the house?
options: Mrs. Shamnath
The servant
The mother
questions.correct: The mother

questions.question: Mr. Shamnath would be receiving ____ as his guests.
options: The boss and his family
The boss and his American colleagues
The boss and his Indian colleagues
questions.correct: The boss and his Indian colleagues

questions.question: Who was a stickler for discipline in the house and was meticulous about the smallest detail?
options: Mrs. Shamnath
Mr. Shamnath
The mother
questions.correct: Mr. Shamnath

questions.question: Mr. Shamnath told his mother to wear ____
options: A white saree
White kameez and salwar
A pink saree
questions.correct: White kameez and salwar

questions.question: Who among the following liked Indian dishes?
options: The Memsahib
The Sahib
The Indian colleagues
questions.correct: The Sahib

questions.question: What did the boss's wife wear to the dinner party?
options: A black gown and a gold necklace
A white gown and a diamond necklace
A black gown and a white pearl necklace
questions.correct: A black gown and a white pearl necklace

questions.question: Why did Shamnath not want to send the mother to their neighbours?
options: Because they weren't nice people
Because then the neighbours would keep coming to their house
Because the neighbors weren't home
questions.correct: Because then the neighbours would keep coming to their house

questions.question: Who got up awkwardly and stood before the guests with downcast eyes?
options: The mother
Mr. Shamnath
Mrs. Shamnath
questions.correct: The mother

questions.question: Mr. Shamnath was furious when his mother ____
options: Had no bangles on
Placed her left hand in the Sahib's right hand
Wore black kameez and salwar
questions.correct: Placed her left hand in the Sahib's right hand

questions.question: Who liked village folk?
options: The boss
The boss's wife
Mrs. Shamnath
questions.correct: The boss

questions.question: The mother, in a feeble cracked voice, started singing ____
options: A pomegranate song
An old wedding song
An old folk song
questions.correct: An old wedding song

questions.question: Who had introduced a new note into the party?
options: The mother
Mrs. Shamnath
The boss's wife
questions.correct: The mother

questions.question: According to Mr. Shamnath, in Punjabi homes women made ____
options: Phulkari
Dolls
Pickle
questions.correct: Phulkari

questions.question: The mother sang in a ____
options: Sweet voice
Deep voice
Feeble cracked voice
questions.correct: Feeble cracked voice

questions.question: What was the mother holding in her right hand?
options: A book
A phone
Beads
questions.correct: Beads

questions.question: What is a phulkari?
options: A sort of embroidered piece of cloth
A type of Indian dish
A type of decorative piece for the home
questions.correct: A sort of embroidered piece of cloth

questions.question: Shamnath's boss was a/an ____
options: German
British
American
questions.correct: American

questions.question: The boss asks ____ to sing an old folk song.
options: Mrs. Shamnath
The mother
Mr. Shamnath
questions.correct: The mother

questions.question: 'Suddenly a problem reared up before Shamnath.' What problem is referred to here?
options: The mother
The food arrangements
The seating plan
questions.correct: The mother

questions.question: Why did the mother not want to make a phulkari for the boss?
options: She didn't like the boss
She didn't know how to make one
Her eyesight was feeble
questions.correct: Her eyesight was feeble

questions.question: Where does the mother want to be sent?
options: Her brother's house
The neighbors' house
Hardwar
questions.correct: Hardwar

questions.question: By what time had Mr. and Mrs. Shamnath arranged everything for the party?
options: By 5 o'clock
By 6 o'clock
By 8 o'clock
questions.correct: By 5 o'clock

questions.question: Why did the mother sell her jewelry?
options: For daily cash expenses
For her son's marriage
For her son's education
questions.correct: For her son's education

questions.question: What kind of a person was the mother?
options: Highly educated
Old-fashioned
Greedy
questions.correct: Old-fashioned

questions.question: Why didn't Mr. and Mrs. Shamnath want the mother to appear before the guests?
options: Because she was unwell
Because she was an old-fashioned lady
Because she was rude to strangers
questions.correct: Because she was an old-fashioned lady

questions.question: Why does the mother agree to make a phulkari in the end?
options: Mr. Shamnath forced her
Because she genuinely wanted to
Because her son stood a chance for a raise or promotion
questions.correct: Because her son stood a chance for a raise or promotion

questions.question: Why didn't Shamnath want to send his mother to Hardwar?
options: Because she'd be lonely there
Because people would say he couldn't even give shelter to his own mother
Because they didn't have enough money
questions.correct: Because people would say he couldn't even give shelter to his own mother

questions.question: What song does Shamnath suggest his mother sing for the guests?
options: An old folk song
The pomegranate song
He didn't suggest any song
questions.correct: The pomegranate song

questions.question: The mother refuses to eat dinner because ____
options: She wasn't hungry
She was fasting for a religious occasion
She did not eat when flesh was cooked in the house
questions.correct: She did not eat when flesh was cooked in the house

questions.question: Who is the author of 'The Boss Came to Dinner'?
options: Rabindranath Tagore
Jayanta Mahapatra
Bhisham Sahni
Purushottam Lal
questions.correct: Bhisham Sahni

questions.question: There would be about ____ guests, mostly his Indian colleagues and their wives.
How many bangles did the mother have in the story?
options: None
One
Two
Three
questions.correct: None

questions.question: The guests had departed one by one at ____
options: 10 pm
11 pm
Midnight
Evening
questions.correct: Midnight

questions.question: Mr. Shamnath had invited his boss to ____
options: Lunch
Breakfast
Dinner
questions.correct: Dinner

questions.question: 'Bric-a-brac' means ____
options: Bits of furniture, ornaments, etc. of no great value
The framework of a bed
Outward show of prosperity
Small interesting tales
questions.correct: Bits of furniture, ornaments, etc. of no great value
